**Initiating Application Design**

I'm starting the deep dive into the user's requirements.  I've pinpointed the central goal: a bespoke quoting application for a metalworking shop.  My focus is now on extracting the core features needed, like the ability to handle various types of work, pricing structures, and output formats.  I'm also considering potential integrations, such as a customer database.


**Analyzing Functionality & Tech**

I've moved on to fleshing out the application's core functions. Managing articles, clients, and creating/saving quotes are the primary needs. Considering the original Portuguese instructions, the app design must be adaptable. I'm also confirming the React/TypeScript frontend requirements and integrating local storage for data persistence, mimicking backend functionality. Gemini API integration could be considered later for advanced features.


**Defining Frontend Solution**

I'm now clarifying the solution's frontend focus, despite the Python request. The core will be a React web application as I'm a frontend specialist.  I'll ensure functional parity with the original intent - managing Clients, Articles, and Quotes using React with TypeScript. The Gemini API for AI-assisted content generation is a strong possibility, and I'll lean on local storage for persistence.


**Formulating Core Components**

I'm now zeroing in on the critical application components. The `QuoteList` and `QuoteBuilder` are becoming central, handling client selection, item addition, and calculation. I am also planning the `GeminiAssistant` component, a nice touch to create professional descriptions for quotes. UI/UX design is also moving forward with an industrial yet clean theme; dark grays and metallic accents. I'm focusing on responsiveness, catering to both mobile and desktop.


**Mapping App Structure**

I'm now focusing on mapping out the application structure. I've defined core types for `Client`, `Article`, and `Quote` data, and I'm planning out the file organization. I have a `storageService` for local data persistence and a `geminiService` for integration with the Gemini API. The `components/ui` folder holds base elements, and dedicated components for clients, articles, and quotes.  The `App.tsx` file handles routing and layout. The React app is the core.


**Coding Core Functionality**

I'm deep into the coding phase now, building out the essential components. The quote builder will dynamically add and remove rows for items, calculating subtotals as the user inputs information. I'm also planning to leverage the Gemini API for expanding user-provided brief descriptions into more detailed and professional content, triggered by an "AI Assistant" button.  I'm using `gemini-2.5-flash` for the API calls to optimize speed and cost, and using a clean, professional dark gray color palette for the UI. I've defined essential data types for clients, articles, and quotes.


**Integrating Print Functionality**

I'm tackling state management. A context (`DataContext.tsx`) will be used for simpler flow. After verifying imports and null checks, I'll add a print-friendly view using CSS's `@media print`. Next, I'll turn to constructing the XML data block. I'll also integrate Portuguese UI to align with user needs. Finally, I will make the specified files: `metadata.json`, `index.html`.


**Implementing QuoteManager Refinement**

I'm now implementing the `QuoteManager`'s print functionality as described. I'm building a preview/print modal that will be triggered when the user clicks the print button.  I'm using the components defined in the recent steps of planning, namely `types.ts`, `services/geminiService.ts`, and `storage.ts`, to allow users to interact with the printing function. My goal is a usable, print-friendly view.

6.  **`index.html`**: Limpeza dos scripts de CDN e ligação correta ao entry point.
7.  **`index.tsx`**: Importar o ficheiro CSS.

**Como fazer o Deploy no Render:**
1.  Faz upload destes ficheiros para um repositório GitHub/GitLab.
2.  No Render, cria um **Static Site**.
3.  Liga ao teu repositório.
4.  **Build Command:** `npm run build`
5.  **Publish Directory:** `dist`

payload.text:
### 2. Configurações no Render

Acede a [dashboard.render.com](https://dashboard.render.com/), clica em **New +** e seleciona **Static Site**. Liga o teu repositório (GitHub/GitLab) e usa os seguintes dados:

| Campo | O que colocar |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Name** | `serralharia-pro` (ou o nome que preferires) |
| **Branch** | `main` (ou `master`, depende do teu git) |
| **Root Directory** | *(Deixar em branco)* |
| **Build Command** | `npm install && npm run build` |
| **Publish Directory** | `dist` |

### 3. Variáveis de Ambiente (Environment Variables)

Ainda na página de criação (ou na aba "Environment" depois de criar), tens de adicionar a chave da Google Gemini para a IA funcionar:

1.  Clica em **Add Environment Variable**.
2.  **Key:** `API_KEY`
3.  **Value:** *(Cola aqui a tua chave da Google Gemini AI)*

### 4. Rewrites (Redirecionamentos)
Como é uma aplicação de página única (SPA), embora não estejamos a usar rotas complexas na URL, é boa prática garantir que tudo aponta para o `index.html`.
No Render, vai à aba **Redirects/Rewrites**:
*   **Source:** `/*`
*   **Destination:** `/index.html`
*   **Action:** `Rewrite`

Depois disto, clica em **Create Static Site**. O Render vai instalar as dependências, compilar o projeto e em 1-2 minutos terás um link `https://serralharia-pro-xxxx.onrender.com` funcional.

Vou atualizar a aplicação para suportar **Sincronização na Nuvem com Supabase**.
Adicionei uma nova área nas **Configurações** onde poderás ligar o teu projeto Supabase.

### O que tens de fazer (Grátis):
1.  Cria uma conta em [supabase.com](https://supabase.com).
2.  Cria um "New Project".
3.  Vai ao **SQL Editor** no menu esquerdo e cola este código para criar a tabela:
    ```sql
    create table backups (
      id int primary key generated always as identity,
      updated_at timestamptz default now(),
      data jsonb not null
    );
    ```
4.  Vai a **Project Settings > API** e copia o `Project URL` e a `anon public` Key.
5.  Cola esses dados na nova área de **Configurações** da tua aplicação.

Implementei um sistema de **sincronização automática em segundo plano** para resolver o problema dos dados não aparecerem noutros computadores.

**O que foi feito:**
1.  **Sincronização Automática (Upload):** Sempre que fizer uma alteração (editar cliente, criar orçamento, etc.), a aplicação aguarda 3 segundos (para não sobrecarregar) e envia automaticamente os dados para o Supabase, caso as chaves estejam configuradas.
2.  **Sincronização no Início (Download):** Ao abrir a aplicação, se as chaves do Supabase estiverem configuradas no `localStorage`, o sistema verifica automaticamente se existe uma versão mais recente na nuvem e atualiza a aplicação.
3.  **Indicador Visual:** Adicionei um indicador no fundo do menu lateral esquerdo (sidebar) que mostra o estado da sincronização: "A sincronizar...", "Atualizado" ou "Erro", com a hora da última sincronização.
4.  **Campo `lastUpdated`:** Adicionei um campo de data/hora (`lastUpdated`) aos dados para saber qual versão (local ou nuvem) é a mais recente.

**Nota Importante para Novos Computadores:**
Para que funcione num **computador novo (Computador B)**, a primeira vez que abrir a aplicação, esta estará vazia. Terá de:
1.  Entrar com o utilizador `admin` / `1234`.
2.  Ir a **Configurações**.
3.  Inserir o URL e a Chave do Supabase.
4.  Clicar em **Receber (Download)** uma única vez.
5.  A partir daí, a sincronização será automática.

To resolve the issues with the blank second page in the PDF generation and add the "Save PDF" option: 1. **Updated `index.html`**: * Modified the `.print-container` style in `@media print`. It was previously fixed to `height: 297mm !important`, which clipped any content beyond the first page. It is now set to `height: auto !important` and `min-height: 100vh`, allowing `html2pdf` to capture the full length of the document. 2. **Updated `components/QuoteManager.tsx`**: * **Fix Blank Page**: Removed the redundant `
` element between the cover and details. Instead, added the `html2pdf__page-break` class directly to the second page container (`#page2-start`). This provides a cleaner break signal to the library. * **Adjust Cover Height**: Changed the first page container from `h-[297mm]` to `min-h-[290mm]` with `style={{ height: '290mm' }}`. This ensures the first page fills the PDF page without accidentally overflowing by a few pixels and creating an unwanted blank page in between. * **Added "Guardar PDF" Button**: Added a dedicated "Guardar PDF" (Save PDF) button in the main Edit toolbar (next to the status selector). This allows you to generate the PDF directly without going into the preview mode first, although it still briefly uses the print view to render the content. * **Enhanced `handleDownloadPdf`**: Updated the function to support generating the PDF from any view (Edit/List) by temporarily switching to the 'print' view, generating the file, and switching back. These changes ensure the PDF has exactly two pages (Cover + Details) without blank pages in between, and provides a quick way to save it.

entries.description: Fix print container height to allow multiple pages and prevent clipping
Fix PDF generation blank page issue and add explicit PDF button
